title: Variables & Types
description: Master Go's type system — var vs :=, fundamental types, type conversions, and nil. Getting these right prevents subtle bugs and shows interviewers you know idiomatic Go.

sections.title: Declaring variables
sections.explanation: Go is statically typed. Use **var** for explicit declarations and **:=** for short declarations that infer the type. The five types you'll use constantly: **int**, **float64**, **string**, **bool**, and pointers/nil. Short declarations are preferred inside functions for conciseness.
sections.example:
func main() {
    var count int = 0        // explicit type
    ratio := 0.5             // short declare, inferred float64
    name := "target"         // inferred string
    found := false           // inferred bool
    var result *int = nil    // pointer, nil by default

    fmt.Println(count)       // 0
    fmt.Println(ratio)       // 0.5
    fmt.Println(name)        // target
    fmt.Println(found)       // false
    fmt.Println(result)      // <nil>
}

exercise.prompt: Declare five variables using short declaration (:=): `count` (int 42), `ratio` (float64 3.14), `name` (string "alice"), `found` (bool true), and `result` set to nil (use a *int type with var). Print each on its own line.
exercise.starterCode:
func main() {
    count := 
    ratio := 
    name := 
    found := 
    var result *int = 

    fmt.Println(count)
    fmt.Println(ratio)
    fmt.Println(name)
    fmt.Println(found)
    fmt.Println(result)
}

validation.message: Each variable should print its value on a separate line
Use := for short declarations and var for the nil pointer

exercise.hint: count := 42, ratio := 3.14, name := "alice", found := true, var result *int = nil.

sections.title: Type conversion
sections.explanation: Go has no implicit conversions — you must cast explicitly with **T(value)**. Common conversions: **int()** / **float64()** between numeric types, and **strconv.Itoa()** / **strconv.Atoi()** between strings and ints. Atoi returns two values (value, error), which is idiomatic Go.
sections.example:
func main() {
    a := 7
    b := 2
    result := float64(a) / float64(b)
    fmt.Println(result)               // 3.5

    num, _ := strconv.Atoi("42")
    fmt.Println(num + 1)              // 43

    text := strconv.Itoa(256)
    fmt.Println(text)                 // 256
}

exercise.prompt: Convert the string "99" to an int using strconv.Atoi (ignore the error with _). Convert the int 256 to a string using strconv.Itoa. Divide 7 by 2 as float64. Print all three results.
exercise.starterCode:
func main() {
    num, _ := strconv.("99")
    text := strconv.(256)
    result := float64(7) / float64(2)

    fmt.Println(num)
    fmt.Println(text)
    fmt.Println(result)
}

validation.message: Check your conversions — Atoi for string->int, Itoa for int->string
Use strconv.Atoi, strconv.Itoa, and float64() for conversions

exercise.hint: strconv.Atoi("99") returns (99, nil). strconv.Itoa(256) returns "256". float64(7) / float64(2) gives 3.5.

sections.title: Zero values and nil
sections.explanation: In Go, every declared variable gets a **zero value** if not initialized: **0** for numbers, **""** for strings, **false** for bools, and **nil** for pointers, slices, maps, and interfaces. Understanding zero values prevents null-pointer panics and clarifies when you need explicit initialization with `make()`.
sections.example:
func main() {
    var n int
    var f float64
    var s string
    var b bool
    var p *int
    var sl []int

    fmt.Println(n)        // 0
    fmt.Println(f)        // 0
    fmt.Println(s == "")  // true
    fmt.Println(b)        // false
    fmt.Println(p)        // <nil>
    fmt.Println(sl)       // []
    fmt.Println(sl == nil) // true
}

exercise.prompt: Declare variables with var (no initial value): an int `n`, a string `s`, a bool `b`, and a slice `nums` ([]int). Print each, then print whether `s == ""` and whether `nums == nil`.
exercise.starterCode:
func main() {
    var n int
    var s string
    var b bool
    var nums []int

    fmt.Println(n)
    fmt.Println(s)
    fmt.Println(b)
    fmt.Println(nums)
    fmt.Println(s == "")
    fmt.Println(nums == nil)
}

validation.message: Zero values: int is 0, string is empty, bool is false, nil slice prints as []
Use var declarations without initial values to get zero values

exercise.hint: Just declare with var and no value. Go assigns zero values automatically. s == "" is true, nums == nil is true.

sections.title: Constants and iota
sections.explanation: Use **const** for values that never change. Go's **iota** auto-increments within a const block, perfect for enumerations. In interviews, constants are used for MOD arithmetic, infinity approximations, and direction arrays in grid problems.

exercise.prompt: Define a constant MOD as 1000000007. Define an iota block with constants Small, Medium, Large (starting at 0). Print MOD, the result of 123456789 % MOD, and the three size constants.

validation.message: MOD should be 1000000007, and iota starts at 0 incrementing by 1
Use const for MOD and iota for the enumeration

exercise.hint: const MOD = 1000000007. Use Small = iota to start the enumeration at 0. Medium and Large auto-increment.
